title: Written task, reflection and programme notes
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the written task for the IB Music SL Presenting component. It deconstructs the requirements for programme notes and critical reflection, offering strategies and model examples to help students achieve top marks by integrating formal analysis, contextual understanding, and personal engagement.
learningObjectives: Understand the assessment criteria for the Presenting written task (programme notes and reflection).
Learn to structure and write effective programme notes for an educated, non-specialist audience.
Develop the ability to write a critical reflection that connects intentions, processes, and outcomes.
Integrate concepts from the Areas of Inquiry (AoIs) to add depth and context to the written work.
Apply appropriate musical terminology accurately and effectively to support analysis and reflection.

simpleExplanation.title: From Performance to Paper: Mastering Your Written Submission
simpleExplanation.summary: The Presenting component isn't just about your performance; it's also about articulating your understanding. The written task, comprising programme notes and a reflection, is your chance to explain the 'what', 'how', and 'why' behind your music, demonstrating your depth as a musician.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your performance as a gourmet meal you've prepared. The programme notes are the menu description: they tell the diner what the dish is (the piece), its key ingredients (musical features), and its origin (historical context), whetting their appetite. The reflection is like the chef's personal notes on the recipe: it explains why you chose this dish, the challenges you faced in perfecting it (technical/interpretive hurdles), and what you discovered about cooking (your musical journey) in the process.
steps: Deconstruct the Task: Clearly separate the requirements for the programme notes (audience-focused, informative) and the reflection (personal, analytical, evaluative). Note the 600-word limit.
Draft the Programme Notes: Research the piece's context, form, and key characteristics. Write for an intelligent listener, balancing technical detail with accessible explanation.

Craft the Critical Reflection: Connect your personal journey with the music. Discuss your intentions, the specific challenges you overcame, and what you learned, linking your experience to the Areas of Inquiry (AoIs).

Review Against Criteria: Edit your work meticulously. Ensure it is coherent, demonstrates clear musical links, offers critical reflection, and adheres to the word count. Check for accuracy in terminology and grammar.

flashcards.back: A written text for the audience that provides contextual and musical information about the piece(s) being performed. Aims to enhance the listening experience.
A critical evaluation of the student's own musical journey with the chosen repertoire. It connects intentions, processes, and outcomes, and should be linked to the Areas of Inquiry (AoIs).
A key assessment requirement. This involves connecting the music to its context, to other works, to theoretical concepts, or to the student's personal experience and intentions.
Demonstrating awareness of the historical, social, and cultural background of the music, and explaining how this context influences its features and interpretation.
The four course lenses (Music in context, Music for listening and performance, Music and technology, Music and identity) used to frame musical engagement and provide depth to the reflection.

Goes beyond simple description ('I practised scales') to evaluation and synthesis ('Practising chromatic scales in this passage was crucial for achieving the intended sense of frantic energy, a key expressive goal').
A core component of the reflection. The student should state their initial interpretive or technical goals (intention) and evaluate how successfully they were realised in the performance (outcome).
The study of musical structure and design. In programme notes, this might mean identifying the form (e.g., sonata form, AABA) and explaining how the composer uses it.
Assesses the logical flow, clarity, and organisation of the written work. Top-band responses are well-structured, fluent, and effectively organised.

flashcards.front: Criterion B: Musical Links and Contextual Understanding
flashcards.back: Assesses the quality and relevance of connections made. Top-band responses show insightful and well-supported links between the music, its context, and the student's work.
Assesses the depth of evaluation of the student's musical processes and outcomes. Top-band responses are perceptive, self-aware, and demonstrate critical thinking.
The emotional or atmospheric characteristics of the music. In your writing, you must connect specific musical elements (e.g., dynamics, articulation) to the expressive effects they create.

faq.q: What is the most common mistake students make in the written task?
faq.a: The most common mistake is being purely descriptive instead of analytical and critical. For example, describing what happens in the music ('the music gets louder') instead of analysing why ('the crescendo builds tension towards the recapitulation'), or listing practice activities ('I practised scales') instead of reflecting on their impact ('Isolating the scale passages enabled me to achieve the clarity needed for the virtuosic character of the coda').

faq.q: Can I write about a piece I composed myself for the 'Presenting as a Creator' option?
faq.a: Yes, absolutely. The principles are the same. Your programme notes would introduce the audience to your new work, explaining its structure, influences, and sound world. Your reflection would be a critical evaluation of your compositional process, discussing your intentions, the challenges you faced, and how successfully you feel the final piece realised your initial vision.

faq.q: How much musical terminology should I use?
faq.a: Use terminology where it adds precision and demonstrates your knowledge, but always ensure it serves the reader. In the programme notes, if you use a technical term like 'hemiola', briefly explain its effect ('a rhythmic device that creates a feeling of shifting metre'). In the reflection, you can be more technical as the audience is the examiner, but the focus should remain on your personal musical journey, not just a theory exercise.

faq.q: Do I need to cite sources in my programme notes?
faq.a: While this is not a formal research paper and a full bibliography is not required, it is good academic practice to acknowledge the source of specific ideas or quotes if you use them. However, the primary focus should be on your own analysis and synthesis of information, not quoting from books or websites.

sections.content: The Presenting component culminates in a performance, but it is assessed holistically alongside a crucial written submission. This 600-word task, comprising programme notes and a reflection, is not an afterthought. It is your opportunity to demonstrate the depth of your musical understanding, your engagement with the Areas of Inquiry (AoIs), and your capacity for critical self-evaluation. A high-scoring performance can be let down by a superficial written task, while a sophisticated written submission can elevate your entire project, showcasing a mature and insightful musical identity.
Deconstructing the Written Task: Programme Notes and Reflection

The 600-word limit is firm and must be split between two distinct documents: programme notes and a reflection. While they can be written about the same piece, they serve very different functions and are assessed on their own merits. Examiners look for two separate pieces of writing, each with a clear purpose.

items: **Programme Notes (approx. 250-300 words):** These are for your audience. The goal is to enrich their listening experience. You should provide relevant background information on the composer and the piece, but the main focus must be on the music itself. What should they listen for? What makes this piece significant? The tone should be engaging and informative, avoiding overly technical jargon where possible, or explaining it clearly if essential.

**Reflection (approx. 300-350 words):** This is for the examiner. It is a personal and analytical account of your journey with the music. It is not a diary of your practice sessions ('I practised for one hour every day'). Instead, it is a critical evaluation of your work, linking your intentions (what you wanted to achieve) with the process (how you tried to achieve it) and the outcome (how successful you were). This is where you explicitly connect your work to the AoIs.

sections.content: Excellent programme notes move beyond a simple biography of the composer and a list of facts. They guide the listener's ear. The key is to make 'musical links' (Criterion B) by connecting contextual information to tangible musical features. Structure your notes logically: start with a hook, provide context, delve into 2-3 key musical aspects, and conclude with a summary of the piece's character.

sections.question: Write a programme note (approx. 250 words) for a performance of the first movement of Claude Debussy's 'Clair de lune'.
sections.solution:
Claude Debussy’s 'Clair de lune' (1905) is perhaps the most famous movement from his *Suite bergamasque*, yet its familiarity can mask its quiet radicalism. The title, meaning 'moonlight', is borrowed from a poem by Paul Verlaine, and Debussy’s music perfectly captures the poem's dreamlike, melancholic atmosphere, making it a quintessential example of musical Impressionism. 

Rather than developing clear-cut themes in the German tradition, Debussy creates mood through texture and harmony. Listen for the opening motif, a gentle, falling figure that is repeated and varied but never builds to a traditional climax. The piece is in D-flat major, but Debussy uses ambiguous, floating harmonies, frequently employing ninth chords and parallel motion that untether the music from a strong sense of tonal direction. This creates a wash of sound, akin to a painter’s brushstrokes. 

The central section offers a slight increase in animation, with flowing arpeggios creating a shimmering texture, before the serene opening material returns. The challenge for the performer is not technical virtuosity, but sustaining a delicate pianissimo and using subtle rubato to shape the phrases, allowing the music to unfold with a sense of timeless stillness. It is a piece that invites introspection, a sound-painting of a moonlit scene.

sections.content: In the worked example, notice how contextual information (Verlaine's poem, Impressionism) is immediately linked to specific musical features (ambiguous harmony, texture, lack of climax). The final sentence connects performance challenges to the overall expressive goal. This demonstrates a high level of musical understanding.
Crafting a Perceptive and Critical Reflection

The reflection is your space for demonstrating critical thinking (Criterion C). The word 'critical' implies evaluation, not just description. You must analyse your own work, identifying strengths and weaknesses, and justifying the artistic choices you made. A powerful way to structure this is by framing your challenges and discoveries through one or more Areas of Inquiry.

items: **State Your Intention:** What was your primary expressive or interpretive goal for this piece?

**Identify Challenges:** What were the main technical or musical hurdles? Be specific. Instead of 'the rhythm was hard', say 'maintaining the rhythmic integrity of the syncopated left-hand pattern against the lyrical right-hand melody in bar 24 was a significant challenge'.
**Explain Your Process:** What specific strategies did you use to overcome these challenges? (e.g., slow practice, listening to different interpretations, focusing on voicing).
**Evaluate the Outcome:** How successful were you? What did you learn about the piece, your own playing, or music more broadly in the process?

**Link to an AoI:** Explicitly connect your experience. For example, 'This exploration directly relates to the AoI 'Music for listening and performance', as I had to balance historical performance practice with my own personal interpretation.'

sections.question: Write a reflective paragraph (approx. 200 words) on the process of preparing the 'Clair de lune' performance, linking it to an Area of Inquiry.
sections.solution: My primary intention in performing Debussy’s 'Clair de lune' was to capture its ethereal, improvisatory quality, moving beyond a metronomic reading. This connected directly to my study of the AoI 'Music in Context', specifically French Impressionism's departure from rigid Germanic forms. The main challenge was harmonic voicing; Debussy’s ninth chords require careful balancing to create a 'wash' of sound rather than a series of clunky dissonances. To achieve this, I experimented with different weights in my left hand, recording myself to analyse the resulting textures. Initially, my playing was too rhythmically rigid, failing to capture the intended dreamlike state. By listening to recordings by pianists like Arturo Benedetti Michelangeli, I learned to employ a more subtle and fluid rubato, particularly in the arpeggiated central section. This process revealed that achieving a sense of 'freedom' in this piece paradoxically requires immense technical control. The outcome was a performance that, I believe, better reflected the Impressionistic ideal of capturing a fleeting moment, rather than simply executing the notes on the page.

sections.content: Integrating the Areas of Inquiry (AoIs) for Maximum Impact

The AoIs are not just classroom topics; they are the conceptual framework for the entire Music course. Your written task is a prime opportunity to show how you have applied them. Consciously choosing a piece that allows for rich discussion through an AoI can significantly strengthen your submission. For example:

items: **Music in Context:** Choose a piece with a rich historical background, like a Baroque fugue or a protest song. Your reflection can discuss how understanding the context shaped your interpretation.
**Music for Listening and Performance:** Almost any piece fits here. Your reflection can focus on the specific challenges of interpretation, technique, and communication with an audience.

**Music and Technology:** If you are performing a piece using electronics, or even using recording technology to analyse your own practice, you can reflect on the role of technology in your musical process.

**Music and Identity:** If you perform a piece from your own cultural background, or one that explores a particular identity, your reflection can be a powerful statement about the relationship between music and personal or communal identity.

sections.content: Proofread your work multiple times. Grammatical errors and, more importantly, mistakes in musical terminology (e.g., confusing 'harmony' with 'melody', 'rhythm' with 'tempo') undermine your credibility and can prevent you from reaching the top markbands. Ask a peer or teacher to read your work for clarity.
